title: Tears

jokes.text: The thing about Ohio is the weather is completely schizophrenic. We dress you wrong, you're either freezing like a popsicle or you're sweating like a hog.
jokes.setup: Gary is giving a sincere weather briefing about Ohio
jokes.punchline: Or a ballerina or modern dancer — Selina deflects Gary's fussing with an absurd non sequitur, revealing she's not listening
jokes.explanation: Gary's anxious over-preparation and Selina's dismissive non-sequitur establish their dynamic efficiently. 'Ballerina or modern dancer' is an odd deflection that signals Selina's contempt for Gary's fussiness. Solid character introduction but not a showstopper.

jokes.text: Have we heard from the president on the economic forum? Uh, yeah. Last I heard, he was three over par. Oh, forum's not happening. Potus took a golf day.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about a major policy forum
jokes.punchline: Gary's initial non-answer ('three over par') and then the casual reveal that the president blew off an economic forum for golf
jokes.explanation: The structure is sharp — Gary's evasive golf answer lands before the reveal that the forum is simply cancelled. The deadpan delivery of presidential indifference to policy is a consistent Veep theme, efficiently established.

jokes.text: Do you know who he's playing golf with? No, who? No, no, no, who? Oh, you don't know and you want me to find out.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Gary who the president is playing golf with, as if Gary might know
jokes.punchline: Gary thinks it's a riddle, gets confused, then has to have the subtext spelled out for him
jokes.explanation: Gary's confusion — mistaking an indirect instruction for a joke riddle — is perfectly in character. The comedic beat lands on his dawning comprehension. Good character integration: only Gary would need this explained.

jokes.text: Long time no cc.
jokes.setup: Dan greets Will with casual faux-warmth at what is clearly a political standoff
jokes.punchline: 'Long time no cc' — a tech-age riff on 'long time no see,' implying he was excluded from email chains
jokes.explanation: Four words. Perfect economy. The pun on 'cc' (email copy) is unexpected, politically specific, and weaponized as a casual insult — implying Will is irrelevant enough to be dropped from chains. Highly quotable, very Veep in its tech-political vernacular. One of the sharpest one-liners in the episode.

jokes.text: Still up here in Cleveland, huh, will? Just rutting around with the regional swine? Slaving away for Furlong?
jokes.setup: Dan performing condescension to Will at a political event
jokes.punchline: 'Rutting around with the regional swine' — grotesquely vivid animal metaphor for Ohio politics delivered with cheerful malice
jokes.explanation: Dan's metropolitan contempt for Ohio is well-expressed. 'Rutting around with the regional swine' is vivid and specific. Good Dan characterization — his cruelty is dressed up as bonhomie.

jokes.text: Well, I am the senior advisor now. So I get free wi-fi. I'll be going to D.C. A lot more.
jokes.setup: Will is being dunked on by Dan about his provincial position
jokes.punchline: Will's sole comeback for his promotion — free wi-fi — is pathetically small; the sentence rhythm ('A lot more.') makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Will's inadequacy is perfectly captured by his 'perk' being free wi-fi. The bathos is strong — his attempted dignity lands as sadder than silence.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. With that provincial sensibility and that girlish figure, you're not gonna last.
jokes.setup: Will just claimed a minor perk as evidence of success
jokes.punchline: Dan's dismissal combines a legitimate political insult ('provincial sensibility') with the oddly personal 'girlish figure' — the combination is the joke
jokes.explanation: Competent Dan cruelty. The pairing of political and physical insults is vintage Dan, though not among the episode's sharpest.

jokes.text: Ah, I recognize you. You're one of Selina's little gay dwarves, aren't you?
jokes.setup: Furlong sees Dan for the first time in the hotel room
jokes.punchline: Furlong's greeting is a wholesale insult delivered with cheerful familiarity — 'gay dwarves' as a category of Selina's staff
jokes.explanation: Furlong's first line immediately establishes him as an equal-opportunity offender who uses slurs as casual vocabulary. The 'gay dwarves' formulation is absurd and specific enough to be funny while establishing his character immediately. High character integration.

jokes.text: What, are you sappy? Is that it? Preppy? / Actually, my name is Dan Egan.
jokes.setup: Furlong asks if Dan is 'Sappy' — apparently trying to name the dwarf
jokes.punchline: Dan gives his actual name, deflating the Snow White gag; Furlong's 'of course I don't remember that' doubles down on the dismissal
jokes.explanation: The extension of the dwarf bit with invented names ('Sappy,' 'Preppy') plays on Snow White. Dan's dignified self-identification followed by Furlong's total indifference is a solid two-step. Furlong's 'of course I don't remember that' is the sharper half.

jokes.text: She's about as welcome here as a turd in a hot tub.
jokes.setup: Furlong explains why he doesn't want Selina's endorsement
jokes.punchline: 'Turd in a hot tub' — a vulgarly precise image for an unwelcome presence
jokes.explanation: A good Furlongism. The specificity of 'hot tub' (not just a pool or a bathtub) makes it more disgusting and visual. Economy is excellent. It's a vivid, quotable insult in his register.

jokes.text: We're having to bump our silver plates up to gold plates, gold plates are going up to platinum plates. I'd make more money if I installed will here as a full-time gloryhole greeter at a Georgetown gay bar. Which I'm pretty sure he already does part-time. Right, will? / Yes, sir.
jokes.setup: Furlong explains Selina's toxicity is tanking his fundraiser ticket sales
jokes.punchline: The escalating ticket tiers arrive at the absurd gloryhole-greeter calculation; Will's compliant 'Yes, sir' to a question about his own degradation
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest bits. Furlong's financial calculation — silver to gold to platinum to gloryhole — is perfectly structured escalation. The kicker is Will's deadpan 'Yes, sir,' which establishes the full Furlong/Will dynamic in two words. The joke does character work AND gets a huge laugh. High craft.

jokes.text: Send her to the rock and roll hall of fame. First time I took this kid, one look at Kurt Cobain's shoes, he was bawling like a fucking baby. 'It's too soon! It's too soon!'
jokes.setup: Furlong suggests sending Selina elsewhere; pivots to an anecdote about Will
jokes.punchline: Will crying over Kurt Cobain's shoes at the Rock Hall and shouting 'It's too soon!' — absurd, specific, and perfectly demeaning
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. The specificity of 'Kurt Cobain's shoes' (not his guitar, his journal — his SHOES) combined with 'It's too soon!' (Cobain died in 1994, this is set ~2012) makes it absurdly specific and hilarious. It's also a perfect piece of character work — Will as a weeping sycophant. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: 'Not great, admittedly'? Yeah, that should be the title of my fucking memoir.
jokes.setup: Someone delivers news about Martin Collins being un-reassigned with the understated verdict 'not great, admittedly'
jokes.punchline: Selina seizes on the phrase as the perfect summary of her whole career
jokes.explanation: Superb. 'Not Great, Admittedly' as a memoir title is an economical encapsulation of Selina's entire arc — her failures are always qualified, never catastrophic, always with this British-understatement hedge. The line is instantly quotable and deeply character-specific. High craft, high impact.

jokes.text: He's not outside here, is he? Oh, hi! Oh, my! Okay, great. All right, that's all I want to say to him for the rest of my life.
jokes.setup: Selina finds out Martin Collins is back and reacts with dread
jokes.punchline: She turns around mid-sentence and he's right there; her forced 'Oh, hi!' followed immediately by 'that's all I want to say to him for the rest of my life'
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe comedy — the person you're dreading is standing right there. The quick pivot from horror to 'Oh, hi!' to immediate exhaustion ('that's all I want to say') is well-timed. Strong rewatch value as it works better knowing the setup.

jokes.text: 66%. What? Approve. Oh! Sorry, disapprove. Oh. Dis— / You know what? You go ride with the security detail. There's no room for you in this car.
jokes.setup: Approval ratings have just come in; Amy dangles the number with false hope
jokes.punchline: The 66% approve/disapprove reversal is devastating; Selina's exile of the bearer of bad news ('no room for you in this car') is the physical punchline
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed. The setup gives Selina (and audience) a moment of hope before the rug pull, and Selina's petty revenge — banishing Amy from the car — is a perfect character response. The 'Dis—' trailing off as Amy is cut off adds to the cruelty. Strong craft.

jokes.text: I might as well nail a bunch of puppies to the ground and then start running them over and crush their skulls with my campaign bus.
jokes.setup: Furlong explains why Selina's endorsement is politically toxic
jokes.punchline: The puppy-skull-crushing image as a political metaphor — gleefully escalating cruelty that reveals how unhinged Furlong is
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'nailing puppies to the ground' to 'crushing their skulls with my campaign bus' is deliberately horrifying and funny because of the specificity. It's Furlong at his most unhinged — the 'campaign bus' detail politicizes the violence perfectly. Strong craft, very quotable.

jokes.text: All right, well, all due respect, cock nugget, there's been a little too much of this informal understanding going on around here.
jokes.setup: Dan invokes 'informal understanding' about the endorsement deal
jokes.punchline: 'All due respect, cock nugget' — the phrase 'all due respect' followed immediately by 'cock nugget' is the whole joke
jokes.explanation: Economy is near-perfect — 'all due respect' is a polite formula that conventionally precedes a mild objection; substituting 'cock nugget' for whatever noun follows is the joke. The compound noun is also inventive — not a standard insult. Very quotable Furlongism.

jokes.text: And in that capacity, I have a duty to shine a light on any of this kind of backseat, finger-fucking insider bullshit, Dan.
jokes.setup: Furlong invokes his oversight committee role with mock official gravity
jokes.punchline: 'Backseat, finger-fucking insider bullshit' — the sudden descent from official language to graphic compound vulgarity
jokes.explanation: Furlong weaponizing congressional authority with crude language is his signature move. The official framing ('duty to shine a light') makes the vulgarity land harder. Good character integration.

jokes.text: She seems nice. [said about Furlong after he threatens Dan]
jokes.setup: Furlong has just delivered an extended tirade of threats and insults at Dan
jokes.punchline: Will's completely flat 'She seems nice' — misgendering Furlong while performing the ultimate understatement
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfectly timed after an extended Furlong explosion. The misgendering ('she') might be an intentional dig or pure dissociation — either reading works. Economy is exceptional. It's a perfect punctuation beat that makes the preceding rant retroactively funnier.

jokes.text: 'Cause of that smiling secret service guy, Amy, 66% disapprove. That's everyone in America who's awake right now.
jokes.setup: Selina is processing the 66% disapproval figure
jokes.punchline: 'That's everyone in America who's awake right now' — the hyperbolic extrapolation delivered as a logical conclusion
jokes.explanation: Competent Selina self-pity joke. The logic is sound and funny — 66% basically is everyone awake — but it doesn't rise above functional.

jokes.text: How could that many Americans not like me? I think they got it wrong. They counted those numbers wrong or something. Or all those Americans are completely wrong.
jokes.setup: Selina is processing the 66% disapproval rating.
jokes.punchline: She works through three stages of denial — statistical error, then mass American wrongness — with each more grandiose and delusional than the last.
jokes.explanation: A wonderful character moment. The escalation from 'they counted wrong' to 'all Americans are wrong' traces Selina's narcissistic logic perfectly. She can't fathom genuine unpopularity — it must be error, and if not error, mass stupidity. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Why have I been given this pig's blood? / It's tomato juice. It's the Ohio state beverage.
jokes.setup: Someone hands Selina a glass of red liquid at the hotel
jokes.punchline: Selina's Carrie-at-prom framing ('pig's blood') is extreme; the prosaic response ('tomato juice, Ohio state beverage') deflates it
jokes.explanation: The Carrie reference is earned by Selina's sense of persecution. The deadpan correction — including the oddly specific 'Ohio state beverage' — is the kicker. The joke works on two levels: Selina's paranoia and the absurdity of Ohio's state beverage being tomato juice.

jokes.text: Greetings from the clubhouse of power, Amy. You need to know that the White House understands just how toxic the veep is after these latest numbers.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives as White House messenger
jokes.punchline: 'Greetings from the clubhouse of power' — Jonah's self-aggrandizing way of saying 'hi from work'
jokes.explanation: 'Clubhouse of power' is a perfect Jonah phrase — grandiose, slightly childish, self-important. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Do you want to know how toxic she is, Amy? Imagine something small has crawled up a dead cow's ass. And then that small thing actually dies itself. If that dead thing then farted out a sack of eggs, but each individual egg is a smaller, rotting dead thing, that's how toxic she is.
jokes.setup: Jonah is delivering a White House message about Selina's toxicity
jokes.punchline: A multi-stage escalating metaphor of nested death and decay — each beat more revolting and specific than the last
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's finest jokes and one of the show's best. The structure is perfect: dead cow → small thing dies inside it → farted egg sack → each egg is also rotting. Every beat escalates the absurdity while maintaining internal logical consistency. It's baroque grotesquerie that only Jonah would deliver with total sincerity. Exceptional originality, structure, and character integration. Comparable to the vasectomy anchor in its escalation craft.

jokes.text: I know you're under a lot of pressure over there, so if you ever need a shoulder to cry on or a bootie to call on, you have my number...
jokes.setup: Jonah attempts to position himself as a confidant/romantic option for Amy
jokes.punchline: 'A bootie to call on' — Jonah's self-aware deployment of 'booty call' slightly misphrased, delivered earnestly to someone who despises him
jokes.explanation: Jonah's delusion that he has romantic standing with Amy is well-established cringe. 'Bootie to call on' is slightly awkward (intentionally?), which suits him. The bit works as character comedy even if not structurally brilliant.

jokes.text: Golf. You know, men walking around with sticks talking about their careers.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Gary about the president's golf game; Gary tries to cover that he forgot to find out
jokes.punchline: Selina's dismissive definition of golf as 'men walking around with sticks talking about their careers'
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational joke about political golf culture — it's true that political golf is entirely about career networking, not the game. Selina's contempt for the boys' club is expressed economically. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Apologies, ma'am. 'Effed in the b-hole' across all networks.
jokes.setup: Furlong asks Will to summarize what the networks are saying about the disapproval ratings
jokes.punchline: Will delivering 'effed in the b-hole across all networks' with complete professional neutrality, including the censored letters
jokes.explanation: The joke is the formality of reporting vulgar slang as if it's CNN chyron text. Will using the censored abbreviations ('effed,' 'b-hole') in a professional briefing voice is perfectly constructed. 'Across all networks' lends it journalistic weight. Excellent craft — only Will, only in this dynamic.

jokes.text: I'm hearing that your credibility is... / Where is it located, will? / The toilet. / The toilet? / More accurately, the crapper.
jokes.setup: Furlong is softening the blow that Selina's credibility is gone; turns to Will for the right word
jokes.punchline: 'The toilet' — then Selina repeats it in disbelief, and Furlong doubles down: 'More accurately, the crapper'
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent: Furlong can't bring himself to say it directly so he outsources the cruelty to Will; Will says 'toilet'; Selina's stunned echo; and then Furlong's correction — 'more accurately, the crapper' — doubles down instead of softening. The escalation from toilet to crapper is minimal but devastating. High craft.

jokes.text: Who are you? Who is this fucking infant?
jokes.setup: Will is the one who says 'the crapper' — Selina has been subjected to humiliation by someone she doesn't even recognize
jokes.punchline: 'Who is this fucking infant?' — Selina redirecting her humiliation into contempt for Will's youth/status
jokes.explanation: 'Fucking infant' is a vivid diminutive that captures both Will's youth and Selina's wounded pride. She can't attack Furlong directly so she attacks his minion. Good character work.

jokes.text: I don't even like you. / Right. / Right? And now I'm, you know, been told to come here and endorse you. So do you understand how embarrassing this would be for me now not to endorse you?
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to explain her political predicament
jokes.punchline: The logic that she HAS to endorse someone she hates because NOT endorsing after flying out would be MORE embarrassing — the absurdity of political optics laid bare
jokes.explanation: The Catch-22 logic of political endorsements — being forced to endorse someone you despise because withdrawing is worse — is a sharp satire of political theater. Selina's frank 'I don't even like you' followed by the political logic makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Then I will reject the endorsement publically.
jokes.setup: Selina says he can't stop her from endorsing him
jokes.punchline: Furlong threatening to publicly reject an endorsement — an absurd political move that raises the stakes further
jokes.explanation: The absurdist escalation — not just refusing the endorsement but threatening to publicly reject it — reveals how damaged Selina truly is politically. The pettiness of both parties is funny.

jokes.text: I know how to give a motherfucking speech. Don't you patronize me with your no jaw. You congressman no jaw.
jokes.setup: Furlong suggests she give a standard political speech; Selina is already emotional
jokes.punchline: Selina's response descends from legitimate grievance to 'your no jaw' — a physical insult so petty and specific it becomes surreal
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The structure is perfect: she starts with righteous anger ('I know how to give a motherfucking speech'), moves to instructive contempt ('Don't you patronize me'), and then bottoms out at the purely physical, helplessly petty 'with your no jaw. You congressman no jaw.' The repetition and the odd construction ('congressman no jaw') make it absurdist. It reveals Selina's crack-up in the most specific possible way. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: He was just not a nice man.
jokes.setup: Furlong has just left after a brutal, demeaning meeting
jokes.punchline: Selina's completely inadequate summary: 'He was just not a nice man'
jokes.explanation: The understatement after Furlong's tirade lands perfectly. Selina's ability to distill a catastrophic meeting into 'just not a nice man' is both a coping mechanism and a funny deflation. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: You know, my papaw used to say, 'it's always darkest before the storm.' So... / Oh, the dawn, the dawn. 'It's always darkest before the dawn.' And then he would kiss me.
jokes.setup: Gary offers comfort with a misquoted proverb
jokes.punchline: Selina corrects him; Gary's addendum — 'And then he would kiss me' — is a deeply odd, vaguely disturbing overshare that lands as tragicomedy
jokes.explanation: Gary's misquote is already funny; Selina's gentle correction is good. But 'And then he would kiss me' is the real joke — it's Gary's touching/unsettling emotional openness that makes this specific. It hints at Gary's deep neediness and his idealization of care. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Her crying was very authentic and humanizing. / Okay. / I like your thinking. / I actually found it slightly erotic, too. / I take it back.
jokes.setup: The team is spitballing about how to use Selina's crying for political advantage
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'slightly erotic' — Amy's instant 'I take it back' makes it land harder
jokes.explanation: Dan's unfiltered honesty about finding Selina's crying 'slightly erotic' is perfectly in character. Amy's immediate withdrawal of approval ('I take it back') is the efficient punchline. Good comedic economy.

jokes.text: That's why it took me so long to split up with Angie. She'd always cry, I'd always get a hard-on. / Now you've lost me.
jokes.setup: Dan has just said he found Selina's crying erotic; he elaborates with a personal anecdote
jokes.punchline: Mike was following until the hard-on detail — 'Now you've lost me' is the kicker
jokes.explanation: A good extension of the erotic-crying bit. Mike was apparently on board with the crying strategy until the TMI pivot. 'Now you've lost me' implies Mike had no problem with the political manipulation but draws the line at erotic oversharing — a funny moral inversion.

jokes.text: The crying game play, all right? All we've got to do is set her up and let the cameras roll. Then we've just got to tweak the tear nipple.
jokes.setup: Dan is pitching the plan to manufacture a second crying moment
jokes.punchline: 'Tweak the tear nipple' — the grotesque, clinical metaphor for emotional manipulation
jokes.explanation: 'Tweak the tear nipple' is a standout phrase — it reduces Selina's genuine emotion to a mechanical lever that can be physically manipulated. The body-horror connotation (nipple) mixed with the clinical control-room language is very Veep. Highly quotable, strong craft.

jokes.text: How do we make it happen? / Could spray her with pepper spray. / Call her fat. Kidding.
jokes.setup: They need to make Selina cry again on camera
jokes.punchline: 'Could spray her with pepper spray' (Mike's deadpan suggestion) → 'Call her fat. Kidding.' (Dan's self-censored reveal)
jokes.explanation: The escalation from pepper spray to 'call her fat' (and the panicked 'kidding') captures how the team views their boss. The 'kidding' is only partially convincing, which is the joke — Dan almost said the real thing.

jokes.text: It's Catherine, right? The absent mother blubber. / Genius. / Chinese daughter torture. It's a good one.
jokes.setup: They're identifying what made Selina cry — the estrangement from her daughter Catherine
jokes.punchline: 'Chinese daughter torture' — the clinical naming of Selina's genuine emotional wound as a strategic tool
jokes.explanation: The callousness of treating Selina's relationship with her daughter as a 'technique' to be named and deployed is very dark and very Veep. 'Absent mother blubber' is the better phrase but 'Chinese daughter torture' lands as the punchline.

jokes.text: I can't believe we're doing this. / On the other hand, we are currently polling lower than a side of beef with eyes drawn on it, so...
jokes.setup: Amy objects to the cynical plan to manufacture Selina's tears
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'side of beef with eyes drawn on it' as the polling comparator — the specific image of improvised, inanimate candidate that still beats Selina
jokes.explanation: Exceptional image. 'A side of beef with eyes drawn on it' is a specific, visual, and original comparator for a zero-credibility candidate. The 'so...' trailing off implies this justifies any ethical compromise. Highly quotable and original — the drawn-on eyes are what make it special.

jokes.text: What is that, a fax? Is that a message to yourself from 1988?
jokes.setup: Someone in the room receives or checks what appears to be a fax
jokes.punchline: Dan's contemptuous questions mock both the technology and the user's age/relevance
jokes.explanation: Functional tech-era joke at the expense of whoever's using a fax. 'Message to yourself from 1988' is the better half. Not particularly inventive but competent.

jokes.text: Look who's playing golf with the president. / Danny Chung. / Chung. / Shut up.
jokes.setup: The team has been trying to find out who the president is golfing with — revealed to be Danny Chung, a political rival
jokes.punchline: The way each character says 'Chung' with increasing weight, followed by Selina's curt 'Shut up'
jokes.explanation: The comedic rhythm of multiple people echoing 'Chung' is a Veep signature — piling on a name until it becomes absurd. Selina's 'Shut up' without specifying who to is the efficient close. Good structural economy.

jokes.text: 24/7 doesn't leave you much time for your family, your daughter. / Well, I mean, it's a punishing schedule... Can you imagine, Felicia, if I'm tired, imagine how tired the rubber makers are here in Ohio.
jokes.setup: Felicia is pushing Selina toward emotional territory about her daughter — the team is watching hoping she'll cry
jokes.punchline: Selina pivots from personal vulnerability directly to 'the rubber makers here in Ohio' — an almost incomprehensible subject change that is both politically desperate and unintentionally comic
jokes.explanation: The speed and completeness of Selina's deflection from maternal guilt to rubber manufacturing is the joke — and it's a very Veep joke about how politicians weaponize false empathy while entirely avoiding the actual question.

jokes.text: She is magnificent. Making rubber day in and day out.
jokes.setup: Selina is pivoting to rubber manufacturing in her interview
jokes.punchline: Dan's awestruck 'She is magnificent' — applied entirely to the political dodge, not Selina herself — makes the admiration both sincere and horrifying
jokes.explanation: Dan's political-operative admiration for a masterful dodge (which is actually a terrible moment) is a sharp satire of the consultant class. 'Making rubber day in and day out' delivered with reverence is funny.

jokes.text: I feel that Ohio is the rubber ball state, if you think about it. Always bouncing back.
jokes.setup: Selina is attempting to recover with a folksy Ohio metaphor
jokes.punchline: The extremely labored 'rubber ball' metaphor — 'if you think about it' acknowledging her own stretch — followed by the flat 'always bouncing back'
jokes.explanation: The joke is the painful inadequacy of Selina's pivot. 'If you think about it' is a tell — she knows it's a stretch. The cringe comes from watching a politician work very hard for a terrible metaphor.

jokes.text: I don't know what the hell she could have been thinking. / When was the last time you cried twice in one day? / It was a little more recently than you'd think.
jokes.setup: Furlong is mocking Selina's crying; asks the rhetorical question about crying twice in a day
jokes.punchline: Will's unexpectedly personal and vulnerable 'A little more recently than you'd think'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. Will's answer is completely unexpected — Furlong (and the audience) expects dismissal or silence; instead Will reveals vulnerability without elaborating. The economy is perfect: maximum implication per word. It's funny and sad simultaneously, and deepens Will's character enormously. Only Will in this dynamic could land this.

jokes.text: I saw the meltdown. / Congressman, I saw passion. I saw conviction. / I saw a fucking crazy lady. That's what I saw.
jokes.setup: Dan tries to spin Selina's breakdown to Furlong
jokes.punchline: Furlong cuts straight through the spin with 'I saw a fucking crazy lady'
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure (meltdown / passion-conviction / crazy lady) is clean. Dan's spin attempt is efficiently demolished. Furlong's persistence in the face of political consulting is a running joke about his brutal directness.

jokes.text: If she mentions my name, if I get the stamp of approval, the midas touch from Jenny shitfinger, I'm coming for you, pal.
jokes.setup: Furlong threatening Dan about Selina endorsing him
jokes.punchline: 'The midas touch from Jenny shitfinger' — renaming Midas's golden touch as the shit touch, applied to Selina
jokes.explanation: 'Jenny shitfinger' as a Midas inversion — everything she touches turns to shit — is highly inventive. 'Midas touch from Jenny shitfinger' is a sophisticated compound insult. Very quotable, strong originality.

jokes.text: Oh, wow, look at this group. Are they gonna live long enough to make it to election day, right behind me? Probably not. Hello, children who do not vote.
jokes.setup: Selina is greeting two different groups — elderly people behind her and children near her
jokes.punchline: She casually implies the elderly won't survive to vote, then greets children by specifying they can't vote — both groups politically useless to her
jokes.explanation: Selina's inner monologue leaking out is very Veep. The specific political logic — calculating voter mortality among the elderly, dismissing children — reveals her as simultaneously charming and ruthlessly transactional. 'Hello, children who do not vote' is beautifully direct.

jokes.text: I made you a cake with your face on it. / Are you kidding me? Oh! That looks like me after a long day in the Senate.
jokes.setup: A supporter presents Selina with a face cake
jokes.punchline: Selina's backhanded compliment of the cake — 'me after a long day in the Senate' — is self-deprecating while insulting the baker's skill
jokes.explanation: Decent Selina moment — turning a compliment into a mild insult while appearing gracious. Not the episode's sharpest but functional.

jokes.text: Well, there's two types of people. You got grabbers. And then you have the other kind. I don't have a name for that. The ones who don't grab. The grabbers, right, they take the world by the scrotum, okay?
jokes.setup: A platinum donor gets time with Selina to discuss his tax reform ideas
jokes.punchline: His entire philosophy reduces to 'grabbers' vs. nameless non-grabbers; 'take the world by the scrotum' as a grotesque variant of 'by the horns'
jokes.explanation: The donor's philosophy is circular and empty — revealing that this 'platinum' contributor has nothing meaningful to say, which satirizes political fundraising. 'Take the world by the scrotum' is a vivid replacement for the standard idiom.

jokes.text: This is just riveting stuff.
jokes.setup: The donor is delivering his grabbers theory
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'riveting stuff' — completely flat, 100% sarcastic, said directly to his face
jokes.explanation: A nice Selina sarcasm beat. The donor probably doesn't even register the contempt. Good economy.

jokes.text: You'd think with all the money I'm jerking up the wall here, I'd get more than a stuffed chicken breast. / I gave 30,000 bucks, I get a chicken breast. That guy didn't give 500 bucks, he's got a chicken breast. What do I get, stuffing?
jokes.setup: The donor paid $30,000 and is fixated on the food disparity
jokes.punchline: 'All the money I'm jerking up the wall' + the chicken breast equality complaint — 30,000 vs. 500 but both get the same entrée
jokes.explanation: 'Jerking up the wall' is a vivid if odd phrase. The chicken-breast-equality indignation from a $30,000 donor is a satirical point about the gap between donor expectations and reality. Good observational comedy about fundraising events.

jokes.text: Hey. It's good to see you. / Why, you need a lawyer?
jokes.setup: Dan greets Larry with generic warmth
jokes.punchline: Larry's 'Why, you need a lawyer?' — implying that Dan only contacts people when he's in legal trouble
jokes.explanation: Economical and perfectly timed. The joke works because it's both funny and immediately foreshadows that Dan does, in fact, need a lawyer. Good foreshadowing-as-comedy.

jokes.text: You know those dreams where your football coach is screaming at you, you look down, you're dressed like Shirley Temple and then all your teeth fall out? / That's like a Disney version of a congressional committee.
jokes.setup: Larry is explaining what a congressional hearing is like
jokes.punchline: The most terrifying dream scenario is 'a Disney version' — implying reality is incomparably worse
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. The structure is immaculate: the setup describes a classically terrifying dream (coach screaming + Shirley Temple dress + teeth falling out) and then calls it 'a Disney version' — the inversion is perfectly timed. It communicates congressional terror while being enormously quotable. High originality: no one has combined these specific dream elements this way before.

jokes.text: 'Cause everybody wants to be the guy getting the TV sound bite, okay? And if they think you're going down and there's gonna be a kill, you might get what we call corpse fucking.
jokes.setup: Larry explains congressional grandstanding — if you're vulnerable, everyone piles on
jokes.punchline: 'Corpse fucking' as the technical term for political pile-on, delivered matter-of-factly as industry jargon
jokes.explanation: 'Corpse fucking' as industry jargon delivered with complete professional normalcy is very dark and very funny. The term is vivid and original, and Larry explaining it as matter-of-fact ('that's what it's called') treats an outrageous concept as routine. High originality.

jokes.text: Sir, this crying is tracking pretty well. / What? / Oh, shit. / Yeah, it's good. / Okay, that changes everything.
jokes.setup: Furlong has spent the entire episode trying to prevent the endorsement; now Mike reveals the crying is polling well
jokes.punchline: Furlong's instant reversal — 'that changes everything' — reveals all his stated principles are purely transactional
jokes.explanation: The perfect political satire beat — all of Furlong's objections evaporate the instant polling turns favorable. Economy is good; the laugh is in the speed of the reversal.

jokes.text: Come on, what are you waiting for? Give her the signal. / What the fuck is the signal? / There's no international signal to endorse. Use your fucking head. Give her a thumbs up or something.
jokes.setup: They need to signal Selina to endorse from across a room at a live event
jokes.punchline: 'There's no international signal to endorse' — the absurdity that they improvised a plan with no agreed-upon signal
jokes.explanation: 'There's no international signal to endorse' is a good absurdist observation that perfectly captures the chaos of the Veep team. The premise that this entire plan hinges on an improvised thumbs-up is funny.

jokes.text: Well, I'm a political leper, and I'm an emotional time bomb. So here's an idea — let's put me onstage.
jokes.setup: Someone asks how Selina is feeling before she goes onstage to give a speech.
jokes.punchline: Selina's self-assessment — 'political leper' and 'emotional time bomb' — delivered deadpan before suggesting the obvious terrible idea of putting herself onstage.
jokes.explanation: A great Selina line. The self-aware diagnosis is delivered in perfect Veep rhythm, and 'So here's an idea — let's put me onstage' turns the self-awareness into absurdist surrender. The two-part diagnosis followed by the counterintuitive suggestion is well-constructed. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: She can't cry three times in a day. That's three strikes and you're out. / How big are her tear ducts? / It's like they're fucking tidal.
jokes.setup: Selina starts crying again — a third time — at the podium
jokes.punchline: The baseball metaphor ('three strikes') followed by 'How big are her tear ducts? / It's like they're fucking tidal'
jokes.explanation: 'Fucking tidal' is a vivid, unexpected adjective for tear ducts. The baseball metaphor is functional setup. Good rapid-fire exchange.

jokes.text: I'm so glad to be here in the 17th state of the union. My grandfather, he was from Cincinnati... We used to say we're awfully embarrassed by our buckteeth. But we were mighty proud of our buckeyes.
jokes.setup: Selina is giving her speech, trying not to cry, and reaching for Ohio bonding material
jokes.punchline: The bucktooth/buckeye pun is so labored and strange that it circles back to being funny; '17th state of the union' (Ohio is the 17th state) sounds accidentally like a weird gaffe
jokes.explanation: The bucktooth/buckeye pun is intentionally groan-worthy — a bad speech moment that Selina thinks is charming. 'Awfully embarrassed by our buckteeth' is the better line. The cringe comes from watching a politician deploy terrible material with misplaced confidence.

jokes.text: Okay, endorse. Endorse. Endorse. / You're sure? / Endorse. Yes.
jokes.setup: The team keeps flip-flopping on whether to endorse based on minute-by-minute polling and tear count
jokes.punchline: The staccato 'Endorse. Endorse. Endorse.' — the decision delivered with the urgency of a medical instruction
jokes.explanation: The comedic value here is structural — the absurdity of this many reversals on such a fundamental decision. The moment is funny in context more than in isolation.

jokes.text: What is that, Popeye? / No, be strong.
jokes.setup: Selina's face is crumpling on stage again and the team is watching in panic
jokes.punchline: Dan calling Selina's expression 'Popeye' — presumably a squinting, grimacing face — is vivid; Mike's 'No, be strong' addressed to the TV is the kicker
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient gag. 'Popeye' as a descriptor for Selina's cry-face is inventive. Mike talking to the screen is a good character beat.

jokes.text: What the hell just happened? / So great to be part of your feel-good moment. / You're a dirty little prick.
jokes.setup: The endorsement has just happened chaotically; Dan takes credit
jokes.punchline: Furlong's 'you're a dirty little prick' — not angry, almost affectionate, as Dan's summary of events is technically correct but infuriating
jokes.explanation: The casual delivery of 'dirty little prick' as almost a compliment — acknowledging Dan's political agility — is a good Furlong moment. Economy is good.

jokes.text: Don't forget your booster seat. / Oh, shove it up your ass.
jokes.setup: Furlong and Selina are parting after the endorsement
jokes.punchline: 'Don't forget your booster seat' — Furlong implying Selina is child-sized; 'shove it up your ass' is Selina's exit line
jokes.explanation: A good closing exchange for these two — both petty, both delivered with practiced ease. 'Booster seat' is a specific insult; Selina's response is the only possible response.

jokes.text: Hey, that photographer who reads lips, he's not here, is he? / Mm-mmm. / No. That's a blessing.
jokes.setup: Selina and Furlong have just been exchanging profanity onstage
jokes.punchline: The reveal that there IS a lip-reading photographer they worry about — and the relief that he wasn't present for this exchange
jokes.explanation: A nice gag about the paranoia of political life — there are lip-reading photographers and you know them by name. The relief in 'that's a blessing' is understated and funny.

jokes.text: You know, my mom said that Selina looked human and vulnerable. / Great. / Not a compliment. / She sees human vulnerability as a weakness. / I like your mom. / Don't talk about my mother, Dan.
jokes.setup: Amy relays positive press about Selina; the reveal that 'human and vulnerable' is meant as a criticism by Amy's mother
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'I like your mom' followed by Amy's sharp 'Don't talk about my mother, Dan'
jokes.explanation: A good multi-beat exchange. Amy's mother seeing vulnerability as weakness tells us something about Amy. Dan's appreciation being immediately weaponized against Amy ('don't talk about my mother, Dan') is sharp and character-specific. Good economy.

jokes.text: I've seen it, I've read it, I've eaten it, I've showered in it. I'm back, my babies.
jokes.setup: Staff ask if Selina has seen a positive newspaper coverage
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of ways she's consumed the newspaper ('eaten it, showered in it') reveals manic relief
jokes.explanation: The escalation ('seen, read, eaten, showered in') captures Selina's desperate relief at any positive press. 'My babies' at the end shows her performing warmth she doesn't quite feel. The list structure works well rhythmically.

jokes.text: Yeah, you are. Like rubber Jesus.
jokes.setup: Selina declares 'I'm back, my babies'
jokes.punchline: 'Like rubber Jesus' — combining the Ohio rubber metaphor with a resurrection comparison, for a character who just suffered and bounced back
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. Two words do multiple things: they call back to Selina's labored 'rubber ball state' metaphor, invoke Christ's resurrection (appropriate given her political resurrection), and reduce the whole thing to a profane, comic image. Perfect economy — 9.5/10. Among the episode's very best lines. 'Rubber Jesus' stands alone as a phrase.

jokes.text: Potus is so excited about this new narrative. / I engineered Selina crying on camera. I'm the puppet master. I'm Pinocchio.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives with White House praise; Mike immediately claims credit
jokes.punchline: Mike says he's the 'puppet master' and then immediately says 'I'm Pinocchio' — the wrong reference entirely
jokes.explanation: Mike confusing puppet master with puppet is a perfect character joke — his self-aggrandizement is undercut by his own reference being wrong. The joke is in the confidence with which he makes the error.

jokes.text: Pinocchio wasn't the puppet master, Mike. He was the puppet. / Uh, yeah, he was. / Five-year-olds get that.
jokes.setup: Mike has just claimed to be Pinocchio as if that means puppet master
jokes.punchline: Dan's correction, Mike's stubborn denial ('uh, yeah, he was'), Amy's devastating 'Five-year-olds get that'
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure is immaculate: correction → Mike doubling down (the character move) → Amy's annihilating 'five-year-olds get that.' The escalation from Dan (who tries to explain) to Amy (who obliterates) is perfectly calibrated. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: I come bearing a message of support from the president. / Oh. / As you know, he called. / Sue, did the president call? / No. / Huh. / Well, it wasn't an actual call. / I am here in lieu of that call.
jokes.setup: Jonah claims the president called with support
jokes.punchline: Sue's simple 'No' punctures the lie; Jonah's face-saving 'I am here in lieu of that call' makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Brilliant. The structure is perfect: Jonah claims the president called → Selina checks publicly with Sue → 'No' → Jonah's backtrack escalates into the magnificent 'I am here in lieu of that call.' The phrase 'in lieu of that call' is an astoundingly pompous way to admit the president didn't care enough to actually call. Sue's role is essential — deadpan destroyer. Very high craft.

jokes.text: Oh, Mike. I am sorry for your loss. / Dan, you're getting a promotion.
jokes.setup: Selina is about to announce a realignment
jokes.punchline: Pre-emptively eulogizing Mike before even announcing Dan's promotion — the funeral framing
jokes.explanation: Selina offering condolences before firing (demoting) Mike is darkly funny. The sequence — 'sorry for your loss' → 'Dan, you're getting a promotion' — is clean and efficient. Good character integration: only Selina would frame professional displacement as a death.

jokes.text: I just signed for an auto lease. If I could get six months...
jokes.setup: Dan is being offered the promotion
jokes.punchline: His concern isn't the job but whether he can get six months of job security to cover a car lease
jokes.explanation: A nice character deflation — Dan's big promotion moment interrupted by his car lease anxiety. Makes him human in a funny way.

jokes.text: Thank you, ma'am. This is all I've ever wanted. / This is all I've never wanted.
jokes.setup: Dan thanks Selina for the promotion
jokes.punchline: Mike immediately echoes with the negative: 'This is all I've never wanted' — perfect bitter mirror image
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. The mirror structure — Dan's sincere thanks immediately inverted by Mike's identical but negated sentence — is brilliant construction. The joke is entirely in the echo, requiring nothing beyond the precise parallel. Could only work with Mike as the loser and Dan as the winner in this exact dynamic. Very high craft and economy.

jokes.text: Is he... is Dan my boss now? / Can I just say that this is really exciting to watch?
jokes.setup: The power shift is happening and Mike is confused/devastated
jokes.punchline: Amy's cheerful 'Can I just say that this is really exciting to watch?' — detached, slightly sadistic observation
jokes.explanation: Amy's observer role here is funny — she's clearly enjoying watching Dan and Mike wrestle for position. 'Exciting to watch' is deadpan Amy at her best, though not her sharpest.

jokes.text: I guess I've got some business to attend to. / What business? / That's a need-to-know basis, Mike, and you don't have the clearance. / Funny. Is that true?
jokes.setup: Dan is immediately exercising power over Mike with vague 'business'
jokes.punchline: Mike asking Selina if Dan's clearance claim is true; Selina's non-answer 'Funny' suggests even she doesn't know or care
jokes.explanation: Competent bit about Dan's immediate power play. Mike appealing to Selina and Selina's indifference is a good character moment, though not outstanding.

jokes.text: This is sad. You're like an old panda that's gonna get put down at the zoo. They bring kids in to watch that.
jokes.setup: Mike is visibly suffering from being demoted in front of the whole team
jokes.punchline: 'Old panda getting put down at the zoo / They bring kids in to watch that' — applying the zoo-panda-execution spectacle to Mike's professional humiliation
jokes.explanation: The panda euthanasia-as-spectacle metaphor is dark and original. 'They bring kids in to watch that' — implying the team is the children watching Mike's professional death — is a vivid, cruel extension of the image. High originality and character integration.

jokes.text: Amy, you pretended to have a miscarriage for me. You remain crucial to my policy team.
jokes.setup: Selina is reassuring Amy she still has her job
jokes.punchline: The basis for Amy's job security being that she 'pretended to have a miscarriage' — an enormous dark reveal delivered with complete casualness
jokes.explanation: The offhand mention of a faked miscarriage as a measure of loyalty is shocking and funny simultaneously. The juxtaposition of 'pretended to have a miscarriage' with 'you remain crucial to my policy team' — the clinical tone — makes it darker. This is Veep at its most politically grotesque. High craft: it's a throwaway line that does enormous character work.

jokes.text: Amy, you pretended to have a miscarriage for me. You remain crucial to my policy team.
jokes.setup: Selina is reassuring Amy that her job is safe despite Dan's promotion.
jokes.punchline: The reason Amy keeps her job: she once pretended to have a miscarriage for Selina. This is delivered as a compliment.
jokes.explanation: A standout line and one of the episode's best. The casual revelation that Amy once faked a miscarriage as a work duty — and that this is the basis for job security — is darkly hilarious and entirely believable in this world. The delivery as a sincere professional compliment makes it perfect. Strong character integration: this could only happen between these two characters in this show.

jokes.text: Don't give me that sad orphan face. Take your little snack pack and your water bottle and get the fuck out.
jokes.setup: Dan is directing someone to move from his new desk area
jokes.punchline: 'Little snack pack and your water bottle' — the specificity of the pathetic desk possessions being itemized before the dismissal
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'snack pack and water bottle' makes this funnier — they're the most pathetically minor possessions, as if this person's entire office life fits in a lunch bag. 'Sad orphan face' is also a vivid phrase.

jokes.text: Congrats on the big promotion. You and I should grab another drink. / You know what? Run it by my secretary. His name is Mike.
jokes.setup: Will congratulates Dan
jokes.punchline: Dan directing Will to schedule through 'my secretary' — who is Mike, his former boss and current nemesis
jokes.explanation: Elegant. Dan directing people to go through Mike as his 'secretary' is not only a power move but it weaponizes Mike's demotion as a running humiliation. The specificity of 'his name is Mike' (as if Will doesn't know) is a nice touch of contemptuous formality. Good structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Relax, cow eyes. I'm not gonna be sitting here looking at your... cow eyes the whole time.
jokes.setup: Mike protests the desk placement
jokes.punchline: 'Cow eyes' is an odd, specific animal insult; Dan stalling and then repeating it ('cow eyes the whole time') lands better than the first instance
jokes.explanation: 'Cow eyes' is an unusual enough insult to work, but the joke relies on repetition more than craft. Good Dan energy but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Twitter eruption. Veep's trending. / Amy, what's going on? / Um, you're trending, ma'am. / I'm on Twitter, but it wants me to create a new account. I don't know how to do that.
jokes.setup: A social media crisis is breaking about the manufactured crying
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to trending on Twitter is admitting she can't create an account — while in the middle of a crisis
jokes.explanation: A good bit about political figures and social media incompetence. The gap between 'you're trending' (crisis) and 'I can't create an account' (her problem) is very Selina. Not the sharpest but characteristically funny.

jokes.text: 'Got played by team veep. They wanted me to make her cry.' Hashtag FakeVeepWeep.
jokes.setup: The Ohio reporter has tweeted about the manufactured crying plan
jokes.punchline: 'FakeVeepWeep' as a hashtag — the alliteration and portmanteau capturing the absurdity of a political weeping scandal
jokes.explanation: 'FakeVeepWeep' is a genuinely good invented hashtag — rhythmic, alliterative, and perfectly descriptive. The fact that a reporter coined it and it's now a national story is a nice satirical touch.

jokes.text: Mike engineered your crying, ma'am. He said that he was your puppet master. / Actually, he said he was your Pinocchio, but I think he meant Geppetto, who was actually the puppet master.
jokes.setup: The tweet crisis is being explained; Dan corrects Amy's recounting of Mike's analogy
jokes.punchline: Dan pedantically corrects 'Pinocchio' to 'Geppetto' mid-crisis — prioritizing narrative accuracy over the actual problem
jokes.explanation: The callback lands well because it's the third beat of the Pinocchio joke — now Dan himself is the one making the correction in the middle of a professional crisis. The irony that the correct term ('Geppetto') is being pedantically inserted into a Twitter scandal discussion is very Veep.

jokes.text: Holy Christ. Look at the size of this one. Buddy, I think you're as big as my gay dwarf. / I need to see the vice president now. / Sorry, I don't work here. I do work in the West... / Well, then get the hell out of my way, you leaning tower of... / Pisa. / No. / Shit? / Good. / Move it.
jokes.setup: Furlong encounters Jonah for the first time and needs to get past him
jokes.punchline: The improvised insult game — Furlong starting a 'leaning tower of...' insult; Jonah helpfully suggesting 'Pisa'; Furlong rejecting it; 'Shit?' being accepted; 'Move it'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional scene. The 'Leaning Tower of...' exchange is brilliantly structured: Jonah earnestly completing the simile with 'Pisa' (cooperative, missing the insult entirely), Furlong rejecting it and accepting 'Shit' instead. The Jonah-completing-his-own-insult gag is original and perfectly executed. The gay-dwarf callback landing on Jonah also works. One of the episode's top three moments.

jokes.text: Hey, sweet cakes. I need to get in to see the veep. / Congressman, if you call me that again, you'll need to see a paramedic.
jokes.setup: Furlong tries to charm/bully Sue
jokes.punchline: Sue's deadpan threat — 'you'll need to see a paramedic' — delivered with Sue's trademark total flatness
jokes.explanation: Sue's 'you'll need to see a paramedic' is perfect character comedy — she doesn't specify what she'll do, just the medical outcome. Furlong vs. Sue is a rare dynamic where Furlong doesn't dominate. The economy of the threat is excellent. Very high character integration — only Sue delivers this line.

jokes.text: The press claims that you intentionally cried in order to divert attention away from the fact that we had to bus people into the fundraiser.
jokes.setup: Furlong has arrived furious; Will reads the accusation
jokes.punchline: The reveal that there was ALSO a busing scandal — they had to bus people in — piling another failure onto the already crowded crisis
jokes.explanation: The busing detail is a good escalation of the disaster — the manufactured crying was itself a distraction from ticket sales problems, which now come out anyway. Veep's chaos-spiral structure is well-executed here.

jokes.text: Number one, congressman, I'm sick of taking your shit, okay? Number two, number one again.
jokes.setup: Selina is giving Furlong a piece of her mind
jokes.punchline: 'Number two, number one again' — Selina's rhetorical build collapses into repetition, revealing she has nothing beyond her first point
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best. The structure is perfect: she builds to 'Number two' — creating expectation of a second distinct point — and then the collapse into 'number one again.' It's simultaneously a rhetorical failure and a pure encapsulation of her frustration. The economy is exceptional. Extremely quotable. Selina-specific in the best way — her rage overrides her political precision.

jokes.text: What are you gonna do? You gonna choke me with some spanx?
jokes.setup: Selina says she doesn't know where her actions will take her and threatens Furlong vaguely
jokes.punchline: Furlong's 'choke me with some spanx' — a gendered, specific, and absurd image of how he imagines Selina's threats manifest
jokes.explanation: The spanx reference is specific and unexpected — spanx as a murder weapon is absurd in the right way. It's a gendered diminishment but the specificity makes it Furlongian rather than just lazy.

jokes.text: And this meeting is now terminated because this was never a meeting to even begin with.
jokes.setup: Selina is ending a confrontational exchange with Furlong.
jokes.punchline: She terminates a meeting by declaring it was never a meeting — a piece of retroactive bureaucratic logic that simultaneously ends and denies the encounter.
jokes.explanation: A good Selina power move. The logic that 'this meeting is terminated because it was never a meeting' is politically familiar — plausible deniability applied to a room full of witnesses — and it's delivered with deadpan authority.

jokes.text: And a bulletproof vest and an iron jockstrap. 'Cause you're gonna get your head shot, your back stabbed, and your nuts danced on.
jokes.setup: Furlong is threatening consequences for Selina's team regarding the Macauley investigation
jokes.punchline: The anatomical breakdown — head shot / back stabbed / nuts danced on — perfectly mapping political betrayals to body parts, with 'nuts danced on' as the escalating final image
jokes.explanation: The structure of this threat is excellent: three parallel items (helmet/vest/jockstrap) map to three threats (head shot/back stabbed/nuts danced on). The escalation from literal violence to the surreal 'nuts danced on' is perfectly calibrated. 'Danced on' rather than 'kicked' or 'crushed' is the creative choice that elevates it. Very quotable Furlong.

jokes.text: All right, you're gonna need to stay in your former position. / In your face, Dan. Mmm!
jokes.setup: Selina reverses Dan's promotion because of the Furlong threat
jokes.punchline: Mike's completely childlike 'In your face, Dan. Mmm!' — a middle-schooler's victory dance from a grown political operative
jokes.explanation: Mike's immaturity on display — his entire victory celebration being 'In your face, Dan. Mmm!' — is a perfect character beat. The 'Mmm!' is the chef's kiss of the joke. Good character integration.

jokes.text: All right, you're gonna need to stay in your former position. / In your face, Dan. Mmm! / Um, ma'am, I may have already issued a press release regarding my promotion.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Dan he must stay in his old position due to Furlong's threat. Mike gloats.
jokes.punchline: Dan reveals the press release he already sent announcing his promotion — making the demotion publicly impossible. Mike's premature 'In your face' is immediately undercut.
jokes.explanation: A good double-reversal. Mike gloats too early, then Dan's press release turns the tables again. The callback to the earlier press release pays off neatly.

jokes.text: Um, ma'am, I may have already issued a press release regarding my promotion. / Really? / Well, can I unpromote you? I don't think so.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to reverse Dan's promotion; Dan reveals he already went public
jokes.punchline: 'Can I unpromote you? I don't think so.' — Selina's resigned logic that a published press release makes demotion impossible
jokes.explanation: The Catch-22 of political press releases — once issued, can't be taken back — is good satire. 'Can I unpromote you? I don't think so' is Selina-specific in its helpless acceptance.

jokes.text: Is he my boss again? / Only in title, all right? You're still senior to him. / In your fucking face.
jokes.setup: Mike is asking if he's still junior to Dan
jokes.punchline: Selina's qualification ('only in title') and Mike's 'In your fucking face' — a second, even more emphatic victory celebration
jokes.explanation: The second Mike victory dance is slightly diminished from the first but still good. 'In your fucking face' escalates the vulgarity, and Selina's 'stop it, please, Mike' caps it well.

jokes.text: Hey, Dan, I'm gonna take a rain check on that beer, okay?
jokes.setup: Will had congratulated Dan on his promotion moments before; Dan is now being un-promoted
jokes.punchline: Will's breezy 'rain check' cancellation — perfectly timed, politically calculating, utterly cheerful
jokes.explanation: Will's political loyalty being entirely tied to Dan's status is a perfectly economical callback. The 'rain check' is a polite, chirpy way to say 'you're dead to me now.' Great economy.

jokes.text: The guy that I have just put in charge of rebutting is now in charge of rebutting questions over the Macauley amendment, which is the very thing he should be nowhere near rebutting.
jokes.setup: Selina is processing the situation she's created
jokes.punchline: The full Catch-22 is spelled out: her solution (Dan for rebuttal) is also her problem (Dan shouldn't be near this particular rebuttal)
jokes.explanation: Selina articulating her own disaster is a consistent Veep move. The repetition of 'rebutting' three times in one sentence is itself comic — she's trapping herself in the word as she traps herself in the situation.

jokes.text: See, now... now I want to cry. But I can't cry. / Can I get you a cup of tea? / Yeah. / You know, I mean, this job has fucked me up so bad. Now I don't fucking know if I can cry anymore.
jokes.setup: The episode's crying arc lands on Selina trying and failing to cry at the end
jokes.punchline: 'This job has fucked me up so bad. Now I don't fucking know if I can cry anymore' — the manufactured crying has broken her real crying mechanism
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic culmination: the cynical use of Selina's tears has destroyed her ability to feel them. 'Now I don't fucking know if I can cry anymore' is both funny and genuinely sad — it's the episode's dark emotional payoff. The absurd profanity ('fucking know if I can cry') in the context of genuine vulnerability is perfectly Veep. Strong craft and payoff.

jokes.text: I wouldn't worry about it, ma'am. / — / I mean, you've only got four years of this, so... / Eight years, I guess, if we got reelected, obviously. / 12 if you run for president. / 16, two-term. / So think about that.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to comfort Selina by noting the job is finite
jokes.punchline: Gary helpfully extending the timeline — 4 → 8 → 12 → 16 years — each increment making it worse; 'So think about that' as a conclusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation comedy. Gary intends comfort; he delivers horror. Each step is logical (two terms VP + two terms president = 16 years) but the cumulative effect is devastating. 'So think about that' is the wrong conclusion to draw — Gary means it as a comfort, the audience hears it as a death sentence. Structure is as close to perfect as this episode gets. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Egan, all right? Dan Egan. That's Dan with a capital D and Egan with an open your fucking ears. Yes, E as in ears.
jokes.setup: Dan is on hold, presumably with a reporter or PR contact, being put on hold
jokes.punchline: Dan spelling out his name with aggressive formality — 'E as in ears' / 'open your fucking ears' — the military-spelling-alphabet tradition deployed as rage
jokes.explanation: 'E as in ears' (not the NATO 'E as in Echo') followed by 'open your fucking ears' is a nice comic circular construction — the mnemonic device is itself the insult. Good economy, good Dan characterization.

jokes.text: Egan, all right? Dan Egan. That's Dan with a capital D and Egan with an open your fucking ears. / Yes, E as in ears.
jokes.setup: Dan is on hold, presumably spelling his name for someone.
jokes.punchline: 'Egan with an open your fucking ears' — a standard NATO phonetic alphabet moment (E as in...) but instead of 'Echo' he delivers an insult. Then corrects to 'E as in ears.'
jokes.explanation: A sharp closing joke. 'Egan — that's E as in open your fucking ears' subverts the phonetic spelling convention with Dan's characteristic aggression, then the correction ('E as in ears') shows him dialing it back just enough to be functional. Very Dan, very economical.

jokes.text: No, she's busy. And tomorrow. / Well, that's tomorrow in the plural. / All of them.
jokes.setup: Sue is blocking callers from Selina at the end of the episode
jokes.punchline: 'Tomorrow in the plural. All of them.' — Sue extending the unavailability to all future tomorrows with bureaucratic finality
jokes.explanation: Sue's final episode joke is exceptional. 'Tomorrow in the plural. All of them.' — the grammatical formality of 'tomorrow in the plural' followed by the total-annihilation 'all of them' — is a perfect Sue line. Economy is 9.5/10. It's funny, character-specific, and the two-word final beat ('all of them') lands like a door closing on the episode. Among the strongest jokes of the episode.

tmdb_overview: In the Season 1 finale, an Ohio gubernatorial candidate balks at Selina's endorsement due to her current unpopularity, but Mike and Amy try to make the best of the vice president's emotions during their meeting. Meanwhile, Dan can't shake the clean-jobs bill debacle, especially when it could be the subject of a Congressional hearing.
insight: Season finale escalates cringe comedy with 61 jokes across relentless character dysfunction.
